13. Take either (i) or (ii).
(i) Let \( f(x) \) be defined for \( a \leq x \leq b \). Assuming appropriate properties of continuity and derivability, prove for \( a<x<b \) that
\[
\frac{\frac{f(x)-f(a)}{x-a}-\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}}{x-b}=\frac{1}{2} f^{\prime \prime}(\xi)
\]
where \( \boldsymbol{\xi} \) is some number between \( \boldsymbol{a} \) and \( \boldsymbol{b} \).
(page 123)
(ii) Calculate the mutual gravitational attraction of two uniform rods, each of mass \( m \) and length \( 2 a \), placed parallel to one another and perpendicular to the line joining their centers at a distance \( b \) apart.

In your answer let \( a \) approach zero, and comment on the form of the result.
solution:
First Solution. Assume \( f \) is continuous on \( [a, b] \) and has a second
derivative at each point of \( (a, b) \). Let \( x \) be fixed with \( a<x<b \). Set
\[
g(t)=\left|\begin{array}{llll}
f(t) & t^{2} & t & 1 \\
f(x) & x^{2} & x & 1 \\
f(a) & a^{2} & a & 1 \\
f(b) & b^{2} & b & 1
\end{array}\right|
\]

Then \( g(a)=g(x)=g(b)=0 \). By the mean value theorem there exist numbers \( \alpha \) and \( \beta \) such that \( a<\alpha<x<\beta<b \) and \( g^{\prime}(\alpha)=g^{\prime}(\beta)=0 \).
Applying the mean value theorem once again, we see that there is a num. ber \( \xi \) such that \( \alpha<\xi<\beta \) and \( g^{\prime \prime}(\xi)=0 \). Evidently, \( \xi \) is between \( a \) and b.
\[
g^{\prime \prime}(\xi)=\left|\begin{array}{llll}
f^{\prime \prime}(\xi) & 2 & 0 & 0 \\
f(x) & x^{2} & x & 1 \\
f(a) & a^{2} & a & 1 \\
f(b) & b^{2} & b & 1
\end{array}\right|=0 .
\]

Expanding the determinant by minors of the first row, we find
\begin{tabular}{rl}
\( \frac{1}{2} f^{\prime \prime}(\xi) \) & \( =\frac{\left|\begin{array}{lll}f(x) & x & 1 \\
f(a) & a & 1 \\
f(b) & b & 1\end{array}\right|}{\left|\begin{array}{lll}x^{2} & x & 1 \\
a^{2} & a & 1 \\
b^{2} & b & 1\end{array}\right|} \) \\
& \( =\frac{\frac{f(x)-f(a)}{x-a}-\frac{f(a)-f(b)}{a-b}}{x-b} \),
\end{tabular}
as required.
Second Solution. Assume \( f \) is continuous on \( [a, b] \) and has a second deriv-
ive at each point of \( (a, b) \). For a fixed \( x \) with \( a<x<b \), let
\[
\lambda=\frac{\frac{f(x)-f(a)}{x-a}-\frac{f(a)-f(b)}{b-a}}{x-b} .
\]

Then
\[
f(x)=f(a)+\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}(x-a)+\lambda(x-a)(x-b) .
\]

Define
\[
h(t)=f(t)-\left\{f(a)+\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}(t-a)+\lambda(t-a)(t-b)\right\}
\]

Then \( h(a)=h(x)=h(b)=0 \), so by the mean value theorem there are numbers \( \alpha \) and \( \beta \) such that \( a<\alpha<x<\beta<b \) and \( h^{\prime}(\alpha)=h^{\prime}(\beta)=0 \).
Then \( h^{\prime \prime} \) vanishes for some number \( \xi \) in \( (\alpha, \beta) \) and hence between \( a \) and \( b \). We have \( h^{\prime \prime}(\xi)=f^{\prime \prime}(\xi)-2 \lambda=0 \), and therefore
\[
\lambda=\frac{1}{2} f^{\prime \prime}(\xi)
\]
as required.
Remark. The auxiliary functions \( g \) and \( h \) in the two solutions are re\( -a)(x-b)(a-b) h(t) \).
First Solution. We first find the vertical component of the force of at
raction between a particle \( P \) of mass \( \mu \) situated at the point \( (h, b) \) and a traction between a particle \( P \) of mass \( \mu \) situated at the point \( (h, b \)
uniform rod of mass \( m \) lying along the \( x \)-axis from \( (0,0) \) to \( (0,2 a) \).
Consider a short segment \( S \) of the rod of length \( \Delta x \) and center at \( Q=(x, 0) \). Let \( \alpha, \beta, \theta \) be the angles shown in the diagram. The mass of
\( S \) is \( m \cdot \Delta x / 2 a \). If the mass of \( S \) were concentrated at \( Q \), the attractive force \( S \) is \( m \cdot \Delta x / 2 a \). f the mass
between \( S \) and \( P \) would be
\[
G \mu \cdot \frac{m}{2 a} \Delta x \cdot \frac{1}{b^{2} \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \theta}
\]
and its vertical component would be
\[
G_{\mu} \cdot \frac{m}{2 a} \Delta x \cdot \frac{1}{b^{2} \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \theta} \cdot \sin \theta,
\]
where \( G \) is the constant of gravitation.
The vertical component of the total attractive force between \( P \) and the rod is therefore
\[
f_{y}=\frac{G \mu m}{2 a b^{2}} \int_{0}^{2 u} \sin ^{3} \theta d x .
\]

Now \( x \) and \( \theta \) are related by \( x+b \cot \theta=h \), so if we change the vari-
\( \boldsymbol{f}_{y}=\frac{\boldsymbol{G} \mu m}{2 a b} \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} \sin \theta d \theta \)
\( =\frac{\boldsymbol{G} \mu m}{2 a b}(\cos \alpha-\cos \beta) \)
\[
=\frac{G \mu m}{2 a b}\left(\frac{h}{\sqrt{h^{2}+b^{2}}}-\frac{h-2 a}{\sqrt{(h-2 a)^{2}+b^{2}}}\right) .
\]

Consider now the two parallel rods, the second running from \( (0, b) \) to \( (2 a, b) \). A short segment of the upper rod of length \( \Delta h \) may be considered
a particle of mass \( m \Delta h / 2 a \), and it follows as above that the vertical component of the force of attraction between the rods is
\[
F_{y}=\frac{G m^{2}}{4 a^{2} b} \int_{0}^{2 a}\left(\frac{h}{\sqrt{h^{2}+b^{2}}}-\frac{h-2 a}{\sqrt{(h-2 a)^{2}+b^{2}}}\right) d h
\]
\( =\frac{G m^{2}}{4 a^{2} b}\left[\sqrt{h^{2}}+\overline{b^{2}}-\sqrt{(h-2 a)^{2}+b^{2}}\right]_{0}^{2 a} \)
\( =\frac{G m^{2}}{2 a^{2} b}\left(\sqrt{4 a^{2}}+\overline{b^{2}}-b\right) \).
It is clear from symmetry that the entire force acts along the line of cen, so the force is given by its vertical component.
Second Solution. Imagine the rods placed as shown, and consider the attraction between an element of length \( \Delta x \) at position \( x \) on the first rod and an element of length \( \Delta w \) at position \( w \) on the second rod.

Since the masses of
tude of the force is
\[
\frac{G m^{2}}{4 a^{2}} \frac{\Delta w \cdot \Delta x}{b^{2}+(w-x)^{2}}
\]
where \( G \) is the gravitational constant. The component in the vertical direction is
\[
\frac{G m^{2} \mathrm{~b}}{4 a^{2}} \frac{\Delta w \cdot \Delta x}{\left[b^{2}+(w-x)^{2}\right]^{3 / 2}} .
\]

The total force in the vertical direction is therefore
\[
F_{y}=\frac{G m^{2} b}{4 a^{2}} \iint \frac{d w d x}{\sqrt{b^{2}+(w-x)^{2}}}
\]
where the integration is over the square \( [-a, a] \times[-a, a] \). Make the change of variables
\[
w=\frac{1}{2}(u-v), \quad x=\frac{1}{2}(u+v),
\]
with Jacobian
\( \frac{\partial(w, x)}{\partial(u, v)}=\frac{1}{2} \).
Then the integral becomes
\[
\frac{G m^{2} b}{8 a^{2}} \iint_{R} \frac{d u d v}{\left(b^{2}+v^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}},
\]
where \( R \) is the diamond-shaped region pictured. The integral is symmetric over the four quadrants, so
\[
\begin{aligned}
F_{y} & =\frac{G m^{2} b}{2 a^{2}} \int_{v=0}^{2 a} \int_{u=0}^{2 a-v} \frac{d u d v}{\left(b^{2}+v^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \\
& =\frac{G m^{2} b}{2 a^{2}} \int_{0}^{2 a} \frac{2 a-v}{\left(b^{2}+v^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} d v .
\end{aligned}
\]

The substitution \( v=b \tan \theta \) reduces this integral to a tractable form, and after some calculation we find an indefinite integral. We have
\[
F_{y}=\frac{G m^{2} b}{2 a^{2}}\left[\frac{2 a v}{b^{2} \sqrt{b^{2}+v^{2}}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{b^{2}+v^{2}}}\right]_{0}^{2 a}
\]
\( =\frac{G m^{2} b}{2 a^{2}}\left[\frac{4 a^{2}}{b^{2} \sqrt{b^{2}+4 a^{2}}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{b^{2}+4 a^{2}}}-\frac{1}{b}\right] \)
\( =\frac{G m^{2}}{2 b a^{2}}\left[\sqrt{b^{2}+4 a^{2}}-b\right] \).
The horizontal component of the force is evidently zero.
First Remark. If \( m \) is held fixed and \( a \) approaches zero, one expects to get the gravitational attraction between two particles each of mass
arated by a distance \( b \). Indeed, using L'Hospital's rule we find that
\[
\lim _{a \rightarrow 0} \frac{G m^{2}}{2 b} \frac{\sqrt{4 a^{2}+b^{2}}-b}{a^{2}}=\lim _{a \rightarrow 0} \frac{G m^{2}}{2 b} \frac{4 a\left(4 a^{2}+b^{2}\right)^{-1 / 2}}{2 a}=\frac{G m^{2}}{b^{2}} .
\]

Second Remark. In both solutions we have treated the rods as limits of arrays of particles. By routine calculations with Riemann sums we could prove rigorously that the integral in (1) is the limit of the vertical components of the forces of attraction between the particle \( P \) and the arrays of
particles that "approximate" the rod. That this limit is the vertical comparticles that "approximate" the rod. That this limit is the vertical com
ponent of the actual force between \( P \) and the rod is an essential postulate of mechanics. For a discussion of this point, see Kellogg, Foundations of Potential Theory, Ungar Publishing Company, 1929, chapter 1.

descriptive_long.question:
13. Take either (i) or (ii).
(i) Let \( mainfunc(abscissa) \) be defined for \( lowerbound \leq abscissa \leq distance \). Assuming appropriate properties of continuity and derivability, prove for \( lowerbound<abscissa<distance \) that
\[
\frac{\frac{mainfunc(abscissa)-mainfunc(lowerbound)}{abscissa-lowerbound}-\frac{mainfunc(distance)-mainfunc(lowerbound)}{distance-lowerbound}}{abscissa-distance}=\frac{1}{2} mainfunc^{\prime \prime}(interpoint)
\]
where \( \boldsymbol{interpoint} \) is some number between \( \boldsymbol{lowerbound} \) and \( \boldsymbol{distance} \).
(page 123)
(ii) Calculate the mutual gravitational attraction of two uniform rods, each of mass \( massvalue \) and length \( 2 lowerbound \), placed parallel to one another and perpendicular to the line joining their centers at a distance \( distance \) apart.

In your answer let \( lowerbound \) approach zero, and comment on the form of the result.
descriptive_long.solution:
First Solution. Assume \( mainfunc \) is continuous on \( [lowerbound, distance] \) and has a second derivative at each point of \( (lowerbound, distance) \). Let \( abscissa \) be fixed with \( lowerbound<abscissa<distance \). Set
\[
auxifunc(timepar)=\left|\begin{array}{llll}
mainfunc(timepar) & timepar^{2} & timepar & 1 \\
mainfunc(abscissa) & abscissa^{2} & abscissa & 1 \\
mainfunc(lowerbound) & lowerbound^{2} & lowerbound & 1 \\
mainfunc(distance) & distance^{2} & distance & 1
\end{array}\right|
\]

Then \( auxifunc(lowerbound)=auxifunc(abscissa)=auxifunc(distance)=0 \). By the mean value theorem there exist numbers \( firstangle \) and \( secondangle \) such that \( lowerbound<firstangle<abscissa<secondangle<distance \) and \( auxifunc^{\prime}(firstangle)=auxifunc^{\prime}(secondangle)=0 \).
Applying the mean value theorem once again, we see that there is a number \( interpoint \) such that \( firstangle<interpoint<secondangle \) and \( auxifunc^{\prime \prime}(interpoint)=0 \). Evidently, \( interpoint \) is between \( lowerbound \) and distance.
\[
auxifunc^{\prime \prime}(interpoint)=\left|\begin{array}{llll}
mainfunc^{\prime \prime}(interpoint) & 2 & 0 & 0 \\
mainfunc(abscissa) & abscissa^{2} & abscissa & 1 \\
mainfunc(lowerbound) & lowerbound^{2} & lowerbound & 1 \\
mainfunc(distance) & distance^{2} & distance & 1
\end{array}\right|=0 .
\]

Expanding the determinant by minors of the first row, we find
\begin{tabular}{rl}
\( \frac{1}{2} mainfunc^{\prime \prime}(interpoint) \) & \( =\frac{\left|\begin{array}{lll}mainfunc(abscissa) & abscissa & 1 \\
mainfunc(lowerbound) & lowerbound & 1 \\
mainfunc(distance) & distance & 1\end{array}\right|}{\left|\begin{array}{lll}abscissa^{2} & abscissa & 1 \\
lowerbound^{2} & lowerbound & 1 \\
distance^{2} & distance & 1\end{array}\right|} \) \\
& \( =\frac{\frac{mainfunc(abscissa)-mainfunc(lowerbound)}{abscissa-lowerbound}-\frac{mainfunc(lowerbound)-mainfunc(distance)}{lowerbound-distance}}{abscissa-distance} \),
\end{tabular}
as required.

Second Solution. Assume \( mainfunc \) is continuous on \( [lowerbound, distance] \) and has a second derivative at each point of \( (lowerbound, distance) \). For a fixed \( abscissa \) with \( lowerbound<abscissa<distance \), let
\[
slopefactor=\frac{\frac{mainfunc(abscissa)-mainfunc(lowerbound)}{abscissa-lowerbound}-\frac{mainfunc(lowerbound)-mainfunc(distance)}{distance-lowerbound}}{abscissa-distance} .
\]

Then
\[
mainfunc(abscissa)=mainfunc(lowerbound)+\frac{mainfunc(distance)-mainfunc(lowerbound)}{distance-lowerbound}(abscissa-lowerbound)+slopefactor(abscissa-lowerbound)(abscissa-distance) .
\]

Define
\[
h(timepar)=mainfunc(timepar)-\left\{mainfunc(lowerbound)+\frac{mainfunc(distance)-mainfunc(lowerbound)}{distance-lowerbound}(timepar-lowerbound)+slopefactor(timepar-lowerbound)(timepar-distance)\right\}
\]

Then \( h(lowerbound)=h(abscissa)=h(distance)=0 \), so by the mean value theorem there are numbers \( firstangle \) and \( secondangle \) such that \( lowerbound<firstangle<abscissa<secondangle<distance \) and \( h^{\prime}(firstangle)=h^{\prime}(secondangle)=0 \).
Then \( h^{\prime \prime} \) vanishes for some number \( interpoint \) in \( (firstangle, secondangle) \) and hence between \( lowerbound \) and \( distance \). We have \( h^{\prime \prime}(interpoint)=mainfunc^{\prime \prime}(interpoint)-2 slopefactor=0 \), and therefore
\[
slopefactor=\frac{1}{2} mainfunc^{\prime \prime}(interpoint)
\]
as required.

Remark. The auxiliary functions \( auxifunc \) and \( h \) in the two solutions are re\( -lowerbound)(abscissa-distance)(lowerbound-distance) h(timepar) \).

First Solution. We first find the vertical component of the force of attraction between a particle \( pointp \) of mass \( smallmass \) situated at the point \( (heightvar, distance) \) and a uniform rod of mass \( massvalue \) lying along the \( x \)-axis from \( (0,0) \) to \( (0,2 lowerbound) \).
Consider a short segment \( segment \) of the rod of length \( \Delta abscissa \) and center at \( pointq=(abscissa, 0) \). Let \( firstangle, secondangle, mainangle \) be the angles shown in the diagram. The mass of
\( segment \) is \( massvalue \cdot \Delta abscissa / 2 lowerbound \). If the mass of \( segment \) were concentrated at \( pointq \), the attractive force between \( segment \) and \( pointp \) would be
\[
gravconst\,smallmass \cdot \frac{massvalue}{2 lowerbound} \Delta abscissa \cdot \frac{1}{distance^{2} \operatorname{cosec}^{2} mainangle}
\]
and its vertical component would be
\[
gravconst_{smallmass} \cdot \frac{massvalue}{2 lowerbound} \Delta abscissa \cdot \frac{1}{distance^{2} \operatorname{cosec}^{2} mainangle} \cdot \sin mainangle,
\]
where \( gravconst \) is the constant of gravitation.
The vertical component of the total attractive force between \( pointp \) and the rod is therefore
\[
mainfunc_{ordinate}=\frac{gravconst\,smallmass\,massvalue}{2 lowerbound distance^{2}} \int_{0}^{2 utility} \sin ^{3} mainangle\, d abscissa .
\]

Now \( abscissa \) and \( mainangle \) are related by \( abscissa+distance \cot mainangle=heightvar \), so if we change the variable we get
\[
\boldsymbol{mainfunc}_{ordinate}=\frac{\boldsymbol{gravconst}\,smallmass\,massvalue}{2 lowerbound distance} \int_{firstangle}^{secondangle} \sin mainangle\, d mainangle
\]
\[=\frac{\boldsymbol{gravconst}\,smallmass\,massvalue}{2 lowerbound distance}(\cos firstangle-\cos secondangle)
\]
\[=\frac{gravconst\,smallmass\,massvalue}{2 lowerbound distance}\left(\frac{heightvar}{\sqrt{heightvar^{2}+distance^{2}}}-\frac{heightvar-2 lowerbound}{\sqrt{(heightvar-2 lowerbound)^{2}+distance^{2}}}\right) .
\]

Consider now the two parallel rods, the second running from \( (0, distance) \) to \( (2 lowerbound, distance) \). A short segment of the upper rod of length \( \Delta heightvar \) may be considered a particle of mass \( massvalue \Delta heightvar / 2 lowerbound \), and it follows as above that the vertical component of the force of attraction between the rods is
\[
totalforce_{ordinate}=\frac{gravconst massvalue^{2}}{4 lowerbound^{2} distance} \int_{0}^{2 lowerbound}\left(\frac{heightvar}{\sqrt{heightvar^{2}+distance^{2}}}-\frac{heightvar-2 lowerbound}{\sqrt{(heightvar-2 lowerbound)^{2}+distance^{2}}}\right) d heightvar
\]
\[=\frac{gravconst massvalue^{2}}{4 lowerbound^{2} distance}\left[\sqrt{heightvar^{2}}+\overline{distance^{2}}-\sqrt{(heightvar-2 lowerbound)^{2}+distance^{2}}\right]_{0}^{2 lowerbound}
\]
\[=\frac{gravconst massvalue^{2}}{2 lowerbound^{2} distance}\left(\sqrt{4 lowerbound^{2}}+\overline{distance^{2}}-distance\right) .\]
It is clear from symmetry that the entire force acts along the line of centres, so the force is given by its vertical component.

Second Solution. Imagine the rods placed as shown, and consider the attraction between an element of length \( \Delta abscissa \) at position \( abscissa \) on the first rod and an element of length \( \Delta widthvar \) at position \( widthvar \) on the second rod.

Since the masses of the two elements are small, the magnitude of the force is
\[
\frac{gravconst massvalue^{2}}{4 lowerbound^{2}} \frac{\Delta widthvar \cdot \Delta abscissa}{distance^{2}+(widthvar-abscissa)^{2}}
\]
where \( gravconst \) is the gravitational constant. The component in the vertical direction is
\[
\frac{gravconst massvalue^{2}\,distance}{4 lowerbound^{2}} \frac{\Delta widthvar \cdot \Delta abscissa}{\left[distance^{2}+(widthvar-abscissa)^{2}\right]^{3 / 2}} .
\]

The total force in the vertical direction is therefore
\[
forcevec_{ordinate}=\frac{gravconst massvalue^{2} distance}{4 lowerbound^{2}} \iint \frac{d widthvar\, d abscissa}{\sqrt{distance^{2}+(widthvar-abscissa)^{2}}}
\]
where the integration is over the square \( [-lowerbound, lowerbound] \times[-lowerbound, lowerbound] \). Make the change of variables
\[
widthvar=\frac{1}{2}(utility-velocity), \quad abscissa=\frac{1}{2}(utility+velocity),
\]
with Jacobian
\( \frac{\partial(widthvar, abscissa)}{\partial(utility, velocity)}=\frac{1}{2} \).
Then the integral becomes
\[
\frac{gravconst massvalue^{2} distance}{8 lowerbound^{2}} \iint_{R} \frac{d utility\, d velocity}{\left(distance^{2}+velocity^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}},
\]
where \( R \) is the diamond-shaped region pictured. The integral is symmetric over the four quadrants, so
\[
\begin{aligned}
forcevec_{ordinate} &=\frac{gravconst massvalue^{2} distance}{2 lowerbound^{2}} \int_{velocity=0}^{2 lowerbound} \int_{utility=0}^{2 lowerbound-velocity} \frac{d utility\, d velocity}{\left(distance^{2}+velocity^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \\
&=\frac{gravconst massvalue^{2} distance}{2 lowerbound^{2}} \int_{0}^{2 lowerbound} \frac{2 lowerbound-velocity}{\left(distance^{2}+velocity^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} d velocity .
\end{aligned}
\]

The substitution \( velocity=distance \tan mainangle \) reduces this integral to a tractable form, and after some calculation we find an indefinite integral. We have
\[
forcevec_{ordinate}=\frac{gravconst massvalue^{2} distance}{2 lowerbound^{2}}\left[\frac{2 lowerbound velocity}{distance^{2} \sqrt{distance^{2}+velocity^{2}}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{distance^{2}+velocity^{2}}}\right]_{0}^{2 lowerbound}
\]
\[=\frac{gravconst massvalue^{2} distance}{2 lowerbound^{2}}\left[\frac{4 lowerbound^{2}}{distance^{2} \sqrt{distance^{2}+4 lowerbound^{2}}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{distance^{2}+4 lowerbound^{2}}}-\frac{1}{distance}\right]
\]
\[=\frac{gravconst massvalue^{2}}{2 distance lowerbound^{2}}\left[\sqrt{distance^{2}+4 lowerbound^{2}}-distance\right] .\]
The horizontal component of the force is evidently zero.

First Remark. If \( massvalue \) is held fixed and \( lowerbound \) approaches zero, one expects to get the gravitational attraction between two particles each of mass separated by a distance \( distance \). Indeed, using L'Hospital's rule we find that
\[
\lim _{lowerbound \rightarrow 0} \frac{gravconst massvalue^{2}}{2 distance} \frac{\sqrt{4 lowerbound^{2}+distance^{2}}-distance}{lowerbound^{2}}=\lim _{lowerbound \rightarrow 0} \frac{gravconst massvalue^{2}}{2 distance} \frac{4 lowerbound\left(4 lowerbound^{2}+distance^{2}\right)^{-1 / 2}}{2 lowerbound}=\frac{gravconst massvalue^{2}}{distance^{2}} .
\]

Second Remark. In both solutions we have treated the rods as limits of arrays of particles. By routine calculations with Riemann sums we could prove rigorously that the integral in (1) is the limit of the vertical components of the forces of attraction between the particle \( pointp \) and the arrays of particles that "approximate" the rod. That this limit is the vertical component of the actual force between \( pointp \) and the rod is an essential postulate of mechanics. For a discussion of this point, see Kellogg, Foundations of Potential Theory, Ungar Publishing Company, 1929, chapter 1.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
13. Take either (i) or (ii).
(i) Let \( radiator(starlotus) \) be defined for \( sandcastle \leq starlotus \leq bluewhale \). Assuming appropriate properties of continuity and derivability, prove for \( sandcastle<starlotus<bluewhale \) that
\[
\frac{\frac{radiator(starlotus)-radiator(sandcastle)}{starlotus-sandcastle}-\frac{radiator(bluewhale)-radiator(sandcastle)}{bluewhale-sandcastle}}{starlotus-bluewhale}=\frac{1}{2} radiator^{\prime \prime}(pinecones)
\]
where \( \boldsymbol{pinecones} \) is some number between \( \boldsymbol{sandcastle} \) and \( \boldsymbol{bluewhale} \).
(page 123)
(ii) Calculate the mutual gravitational attraction of two uniform rods, each of mass \( thundersky \) and length \( 2 sandcastle \), placed parallel to one another and perpendicular to the line joining their centers at a distance \( bluewhale \) apart.

In your answer let \( sandcastle \) approach zero, and comment on the form of the result.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
First Solution. Assume \( radiator \) is continuous on \( [sandcastle, bluewhale] \) and has a second derivative at each point of \( (sandcastle, bluewhale) \). Let \( starlotus \) be fixed with \( sandcastle<starlotus<bluewhale \). Set
\[
labyrinth(raincipher)=\left|\begin{array}{llll}
radiator(raincipher) & raincipher^{2} & raincipher & 1 \\
radiator(starlotus) & starlotus^{2} & starlotus & 1 \\
radiator(sandcastle) & sandcastle^{2} & sandcastle & 1 \\
radiator(bluewhale) & bluewhale^{2} & bluewhale & 1
\end{array}\right|
\]

Then \( labyrinth(sandcastle)=labyrinth(starlotus)=labyrinth(bluewhale)=0 \). By the mean value theorem there exist numbers \( lighthouse \) and \( driftwood \) such that \( sandcastle<lighthouse<starlotus<driftwood<bluewhale \) and \( labyrinth^{\prime}(lighthouse)=labyrinth^{\prime}(driftwood)=0 \).
Applying the mean value theorem once again, we see that there is a number \( pinecones \) such that \( lighthouse<pinecones<driftwood \) and \( labyrinth^{\prime \prime}(pinecones)=0 \). Evidently, \( pinecones \) is between \( sandcastle \) and bluewhale.
\[
labyrinth^{\prime \prime}(pinecones)=\left|\begin{array}{llll}
radiator^{\prime \prime}(pinecones) & 2 & 0 & 0 \\
radiator(starlotus) & starlotus^{2} & starlotus & 1 \\
radiator(sandcastle) & sandcastle^{2} & sandcastle & 1 \\
radiator(bluewhale) & bluewhale^{2} & bluewhale & 1
\end{array}\right|=0 .
\]

Expanding the determinant by minors of the first row, we find
\begin{tabular}{rl}
\( \frac{1}{2} radiator^{\prime \prime}(pinecones) \) & \( =\frac{\left|\begin{array}{lll}radiator(starlotus) & starlotus & 1 \\
radiator(sandcastle) & sandcastle & 1 \\
radiator(bluewhale) & bluewhale & 1\end{array}\right|}{\left|\begin{array}{lll}starlotus^{2} & starlotus & 1 \\
sandcastle^{2} & sandcastle & 1 \\
bluewhale^{2} & bluewhale & 1\end{array}\right|} \) \\
& \( =\frac{\frac{radiator(starlotus)-radiator(sandcastle)}{starlotus-sandcastle}-\frac{radiator(sandcastle)-radiator(bluewhale)}{sandcastle-bluewhale}}{starlotus-bluewhale} \),
\end{tabular}
as required.
Second Solution. Assume \( radiator \) is continuous on \( [sandcastle, bluewhale] \) and has a second deriv-
ive at each point of \( (sandcastle, bluewhale) \). For a fixed \( starlotus \) with \( sandcastle<starlotus<bluewhale \), let
\[
meadowlark=\frac{\frac{radiator(starlotus)-radiator(sandcastle)}{starlotus-sandcastle}-\frac{radiator(sandcastle)-radiator(bluewhale)}{bluewhale-sandcastle}}{starlotus-bluewhale} .
\]

Then
\[
radiator(starlotus)=radiator(sandcastle)+\frac{radiator(bluewhale)-radiator(sandcastle)}{bluewhale-sandcastle}(starlotus-sandcastle)+meadowlark(starlotus-sandcastle)(starlotus-bluewhale) .
\]

Define
\[
foxgloves(raincipher)=radiator(raincipher)-\left\{radiator(sandcastle)+\frac{radiator(bluewhale)-radiator(sandcastle)}{bluewhale-sandcastle}(raincipher-sandcastle)+meadowlark(raincipher-sandcastle)(raincipher-bluewhale)\right\}
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
a_{1}<b_{1},\qquad a_{2}<b_{2},\qquad a_{3}<b_{3},
\]  
and put  
\[
B:=[a_{1},b_{1}]\times[a_{2},b_{2}]\times[a_{3},b_{3}]\subset\mathbf R^{3},\qquad   
\Delta_{i}:=b_{i}-a_{i}\;(i=1,2,3).
\]

Assume  
\[
\text{(i)}\; f\in C^{3}(B^{\circ})\ \text{and}\ f\ \text{is continuous on}\ B;\qquad  
\text{(ii)}\; g:=f_{xyz}\in L^{1}(B).
\]

(The mixed derivative $g$ is continuous on $B^{\circ}$ and integrable on $B$; continuity on $\partial B$ is not required.)

Define the eight-point third-order divided difference  
\[
\begin{aligned}
D(f)=&\,f(b_{1},b_{2},b_{3})-f(b_{1},b_{2},a_{3})-f(b_{1},a_{2},b_{3})-f(a_{1},b_{2},b_{3})\\
     &+f(b_{1},a_{2},a_{3})+f(a_{1},b_{2},a_{3})+f(a_{1},a_{2},b_{3})-f(a_{1},a_{2},a_{3}).
\end{aligned}
\]

Prove that there exists a point  
\[
(\xi_{1},\xi_{2},\xi_{3})\in(a_{1},b_{1})\times(a_{2},b_{2})\times(a_{3},b_{3})
\]
such that  
\[
\boxed{\;
\displaystyle 
\frac{D(f)}{\Delta_{1}\Delta_{2}\Delta_{3}}
      =f_{xyz}(\xi_{1},\xi_{2},\xi_{3})
\;}
\]
(Clairaut's theorem guarantees that the order of the partial derivatives is immaterial.)

You may quote standard facts from multivariable calculus (Fubini, dominated convergence, fundamental theorem of calculus in one variable, intermediate value theorem, etc.) without proof, but every other step must be rigorously justified.  

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  
\[
g:=f_{xyz}\qquad (\text{defined and continuous on }B^{\circ}).
\]

Step 1.  Express $D(f)$ as a triple integral of $g$.  

Fix $(x,y)\in[a_{1},b_{1}]\times[a_{2},b_{2}]$.  The one-variable fundamental
theorem of calculus in the $z$-variable gives  
\[
f(x,y,b_{3})-f(x,y,a_{3})
     =\int_{a_{3}}^{b_{3}}f_{z}(x,y,z)\,dz.
\]
Apply the same procedure to the quantity $f_{z}(x,y,z)$, first in $y$ and then in $x$:

\[
\begin{aligned}
&f_{z}(x,b_{2},z)-f_{z}(x,a_{2},z)
      =\int_{a_{2}}^{b_{2}}f_{yz}(x,s,z)\,ds,\\[4pt]
&f_{yz}(b_{1},s,z)-f_{yz}(a_{1},s,z)
      =\int_{a_{1}}^{b_{1}}f_{xyz}(t,s,z)\,dt.
\end{aligned}
\]
Adding and subtracting the eight boundary values of $f$ and
cancelling the resulting telescoping sums yields  
\[
D(f)=\int_{a_{1}}^{b_{1}}\!\!\int_{a_{2}}^{b_{2}}\!\!\int_{a_{3}}^{b_{3}}
        g(x,y,z)\,dz\,dy\,dx. \tag{1}
\]
Because $g\in L^{1}(B)$ the triple integral is absolutely convergent, so by
Fubini's theorem any rearrangement of the order of integration is
legitimate.

Step 2.  The average value of $g$ over $B$.  

Define  
\[
A:=\frac{1}{\Delta_{1}\Delta_{2}\Delta_{3}}\int_{B}g\,dV.
\]
With (1) we therefore have  
\[
D(f)=\Delta_{1}\Delta_{2}\Delta_{3}\,A. \tag{2}
\]

Step 3.  Produce an interior point where $g=A$.  

The argument proceeds by successively applying the intermediate value
theorem in the three coordinate directions.

(3a)  Averaging in the $y,z$ variables.  

Let  
\[
H(x):=\int_{a_{2}}^{b_{2}}\int_{a_{3}}^{b_{3}}\bigl(g(x,y,z)-A\bigr)\,dz\,dy
      \quad(a_{1}\le x\le b_{1}).
\]
$H$ is continuous (by continuity of $g$ on $B^{\circ}$ and Fubini) and
\[
\int_{a_{1}}^{b_{1}}H(x)\,dx
   =\int_{B}(g-A)\,dV=0.
\]
Hence $H$ attains both non-positive and non-negative values on
$[a_{1},b_{1}]$; by the intermediate value theorem there exists
$x_{0}\in(a_{1},b_{1})$ with $H(x_{0})=0$.

(3b)  Averaging in the remaining $z$ variable.  

For this $x_{0}$ define  
\[
K(y):=\int_{a_{3}}^{b_{3}}\bigl(g(x_{0},y,z)-A\bigr)\,dz
      \quad(a_{2}\le y\le b_{2}).
\]
Again $K$ is continuous,  
\[
\int_{a_{2}}^{b_{2}}K(y)\,dy=H(x_{0})=0,
\]
so there exists
$y_{0}\in(a_{2},b_{2})$ with $K(y_{0})=0$.

(3c)  The final $z$-variable.  

With $x_{0},y_{0}$ fixed, consider  
\[
L(z):=g(x_{0},y_{0},z)-A
      \quad(a_{3}\le z\le b_{3}).
\]
Then $\displaystyle \int_{a_{3}}^{b_{3}}L(z)\,dz=K(y_{0})=0$.  Since $L$ is
continuous, it must vanish somewhere in the open interval:
there exists $z_{0}\in(a_{3},b_{3})$ such that
$L(z_{0})=0$, i.e.  
\[
g(x_{0},y_{0},z_{0})=A. \tag{3}
\]

Set  
\[
\xi:=(\xi_{1},\xi_{2},\xi_{3}):=(x_{0},y_{0},z_{0})\in
     (a_{1},b_{1})\times(a_{2},b_{2})\times(a_{3},b_{3}).
\]

Step 4.  Conclusion.  

Combining (2) with (3) we obtain  
\[
\frac{D(f)}{\Delta_{1}\Delta_{2}\Delta_{3}}=A=g(\xi)
          =f_{xyz}(\xi_{1},\xi_{2},\xi_{3}),
\]
which is the desired statement.

Remark on uniqueness.  
The proof only produces a single interior point where
$g$ attains its average; if $g$ is not constant on $B^{\circ}$ the level
set $\{x\in B^{\circ}:g(x)=A\}$ can be large (even of positive measure), so the
point $\xi$ is generally not unique.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension & more variables:  
   The original kernel needed a one–dimensional C²–function and three sample points.  The enhanced variant moves to ℝ³ and involves the mixed third derivative, demanding control over eight corner evaluations of the function.

2. Additional constraints & deeper theory:  
   Continuity of all third-order partials is required.  The proof must manage repeated applications of the mean value theorem in three independent directions, keep track of the changing “intermediate” points at every stage, and invoke equality of mixed partials (Clairaut’s theorem).

3. More sophisticated structure:  
   The numerator D(f) is an alternating sum analogous to a third-order finite difference on a 3-D lattice; recognising and manipulating this structure is substantially subtler than handling two first-order divided differences in one variable.

4. Multiple interacting concepts:  
   The argument combines classical one–dimensional mean value theorems, multivariable chain rules, barycentric localisation of intermediate points, and the symmetry of mixed derivatives—several layers of reasoning that do not appear in the original exercise.

5. Substantially increased proof length & intricacy:  
   Each new variable introduces an extra level of nested mean–value arguments.  The final identification of the complicated eight-term expression with the triple mixed derivative requires careful bookkeeping, making the overall problem considerably harder than the original second-derivative version.
